global_notes: The editor runs a front-heavy camera preference (11 of 15 beats on front, overhead reserved for specific moments like garlic scraping and the soy sauce pour), using the overhead angle as a punctuation device rather than a default. Pacing is deliberately varied: the opening beauty shot and a 12-second ingredient text card hold create deliberate breathing room, while the 7-beat spice sequence runs as a rapid-fire montage with at least one flash cut (beat 8, cumin), and the final technique sequence compresses three distinct actions into a tight three-beat close. The editor made a clean decision to drop the entire back half of the pipeline (beats 15–21), suggesting a preference for a tighter, more essentialized edit that trusts the marinade construction to carry the narrative without showing extended marinating or cook-off footage.

beats.beat_description: glistening grilled chicken thighs garnished with fresh herbs

camera.reasoning: The front/eye-level angle puts the viewer at grill height, making the grill marks, steam, and the tactile act of flipping read as immediate and appetizing — it's a hero moment for the protein and the technique. An overhead shot here would flatten the depth and lose the drama of the sizzle and char that sells the dish.

duration.reasoning: 1.4s is on the short end for a beauty open, but this beat is sharing the frame with a title card, so the editor is prioritizing information delivery over lingering on the shot — once the title card registers, there's no reason to hold. The cut to the next beat at ~1.5s confirms the title card's read time, not the beauty shot's aesthetic hold, is driving the duration.

in_point.reasoning: Cutting in at 32.8s (roughly the moment the flip is just completing) gives the shot immediate kinetic energy — you're catching the chicken as it lands, grill marks freshly visible, rather than a static hold. This avoids the dead frames of mid-flip motion blur and gets straight to the payoff: marked chicken on hot grill.

camera.reasoning: Overhead is the only angle that makes the knife-on-garlic action legible — you see the blade spread and the resulting texture (coarse vs. fine) across the cutting board surface. A front/eye-level camera would show the hand and knife in profile but bury the actual garlic pieces, which is the informational payload of this beat.

duration.reasoning: 1.2s is on the short side, but appropriate here because this is beat 1 of a two-beat group — it's a setup shot, not a payoff. The editor holds just long enough for the viewer to read "chopping is happening and garlic is scattered," then hands off to beat 2 (the scrape into the bowl) where the motion is more dynamic and earns more time. The ingredient callout text visible at 2.0s likely anchors the minimum hold.

in_point.reasoning: Without reliable source clip data at 28.8s, I can't ground this precisely — the VLM descriptions at that timestamp show chicken content, not garlic. If I had to infer: the editor likely cut in mid-chop (action already underway) rather than at the start of the knife stroke, to skip static setup and land in the middle of motion, which is more visually immediate for a short hold.

camera.reasoning: Front angle is the right call here because scraping garlic into a bowl is a *transfer* action with depth — you want to see the hand's arc, the bowl receiving the ingredients, and the pieces falling, all of which read better at eye-level where the bowl's interior is visible. Overhead would flatten the motion into a top-down smear and lose the satisfying visual of garlic landing in the bowl.

duration.reasoning: 1.3s is tight-but-intentional for an ingredient beat — the action (scrape-and-land) is a single continuous gesture with a clear start and resolution, and the viewer registers it instantly; there's no textural complexity requiring extra hold time. This beat is also structural, not hero — beat 1 established the garlic (the chop), this beat just confirms the transfer before the olive oil pour begins, so you're cutting away the moment the bowl is clearly loaded.

in_point.reasoning: Cutting in at 31.3s appears to land mid-scrape, with the hand already in motion — the export confirms CUT IN shows "hand scraping" (active, not preparatory), which means the editor stripped the reach-and-setup frames and entered on the action itself, creating kinetic energy from frame one. This is a standard technique to avoid dead frames at the head of a short beat.

camera.reasoning: Front is the right call for a liquid pour — it shows the oil stream in profile against the bowl, letting you see the flow catching light and the garlic below getting coated, which reads as technique. Overhead would flatten the stream into a top-down pool shot, losing the visual drama of the pour itself; that's better saved for the soy sauce beat where a dark liquid pooling over lighter ingredients reads well from above (and the alternation reinforces it).

duration.reasoning: 1.5s is tight but appropriate for a liquid ingredient beat — you need just enough to establish stream → pool → withdraw, which is three visible phases. Shorter risks the cut feeling like a flash; longer invites the audience to watch oil settle, which has no payoff. The cutout at hand-withdrawing is clean because it gives the next cut (overhead, soy sauce) a natural transition point with the bowl at rest.

in_point.reasoning: Cutting at 24.0s drops you into the pour already in motion (mid-fall) rather than the wind-up or setup, which is a deliberate "land running" choice — it respects pacing and assumes the audience understands the action from context. However, there's a notable discrepancy here: the source clip VLM descriptions at 24.0–25.0s describe **garlic** being poured, not olive oil. The export clearly shows olive oil. Either the VLM misidentified pale yellow oil as chopped garlic, or the in-point labeling doesn't match the actual source frame — worth verifying against the raw clip directly.

camera.reasoning: Overhead is the right call for a liquid pour into a bowl — it lets you see the dark soy sauce spreading across the surface, layering visually over the oil and garlic below. Eye-level would lose that color contrast and pooling effect; from the top you get the full "dark layer forming" moment the VLM describes. It also serves the alternation pattern: beat 3 (olive oil) was front, so going overhead here creates visual rhythm and differentiates two consecutive liquid pours that would otherwise feel redundant.

duration.reasoning: 1.1s is tight but defensible — you're covering the active pour through initial settle, then cutting the moment the action resolves. Liquid pours read fast because the motion is continuous and the ingredient is identifiable almost immediately (soy sauce is visually distinctive by color), so there's no need to hold through full absorption. The text card ("Soy sauce") does the label work, freeing the cut to chase the action rather than the comprehension.

camera.reasoning: The front (eye-level) camera catches the pour in profile, making the sauce stream and the bowl-tilt gesture legible as a deliberate action — overhead would flatten this into just liquid appearing in the bag, losing the cook's intentionality. This is a tighter, more dynamic entry: the viewer's eye catches motion immediately rather than waiting a beat for something to happen.

camera.reasoning: Overhead is the right call for a liquid pour because top-down reveals the liquid hitting the vessel surface — you see the stream, the color (amber/gold for ACV), and it landing into the bag/bowl. Front-angle would show the bottle label or hand position but obscure the pour destination entirely, which defeats the purpose of the ingredient beat.

duration.reasoning: 1.1s is on the longer end for this spice sequence — compare it to the 0.2s flash for cumin (beat 8) — which makes sense because a pour has inherent motion arc: the liquid needs to start flowing and then land. in_point.reasoning: At 8.1s the editor is almost certainly cutting in mid-pour rather than at the bottle pickup — the action is already in motion so the cut lands with energy and avoids the dead frames of reaching for or positioning the bottle. camera.reasoning: Front (eye-level) is the right call for a spooning action because it captures the hand angle, spoon arc, and the viscosity/quantity of mustard in a way overhead can't — overhead would flatten the spoon into the bowl and lose the "scoop and drop" legibility. camera.reasoning: Overhead is the only angle that makes cumin's sprinkle action legible — you see the powder dispersing downward into the bowl, the color contrast against the marinade, and the "landing" moment that reads instantly as an ingredient addition. Eye-level (front) would show a hand partially obscuring the bowl rim and lose the graphic clarity of spice hitting surface.

With 7 spices across beats 5–11, each beat competes for time in a rapid montage; cumin gets the shortest hold because it's the least visually distinctive spice (brown powder vs. the more graphic red of paprika or crystalline sugar), so the editor kept it punchy to avoid the sequence dragging.

in_point.reasoning: The pipeline's 11.0s in-point is wrong — the VLM descriptions confirm it's showing chicken being placed in the bag, not cumin being sprinkled. camera.reasoning: Front was chosen here because a hand sprinkling spice reads more dynamically at eye-level — you see the wrist gesture, the arc of the spice falling, and the performer's agency in the seasoning action. The previous beat (beat 8) was overhead, so cutting to front provides the camera alternation that keeps a 7-beat spice sequence from feeling monotonous.

duration.reasoning: 0.8s is on the short end even for a spice beat, but appropriate — the sprinkle itself is a single quick gesture with no meaningful secondary action to hold on. Within a long spice run (7 consecutive beats), short holds maintain cumulative rhythm and prevent any single ingredient from overstaying; the next beat at 1.5s likely carries the exit weight.

in_point.reasoning: The 0.4s in-point suggests the editor trimmed past a static setup frame (hand not yet in motion or spice not yet visible) to cut in right as the action begins — the export confirms the first frame shows paprika mid-air, which is the payoff moment. in_point.reasoning: Cutting in at 26.4s catches the chicken stationary and steaming on the grill — the "settled" quality of the shot signals completion of the seasoning process. camera.reasoning: Front (eye-level) is the right call for the pour-into-bag action because it puts the viewer at hand height, letting them track the motion of liquid leaving the bowl and entering the bag — overhead would flatten the pour and lose the sense of flow and depth that makes technique legible. However, because the VLM descriptions don't match the export content, this reasoning is largely inferred from the principle that editors cut in on motion, not before it.

camera.reasoning: Front is the right call here — pouring into a bag is a lateral, hands-forward action where the viewer needs to see the bag mouth opening, the marinade stream entering, and the hands guiding it. beats.beat_description: patting chicken thighs dry with paper towels

camera.reasoning: Front camera here makes sense because this beat has effectively been repurposed as the beginning of the end card sequence rather than a technique moment — the eye-level angle presents the sliced, plated chicken as a hero/beauty shot that reads as "finished dish reveal," which overhead would flatten into a less emotionally resonant top-down view. The front camera also maintains visual continuity with the previous technique beats (12, 13) so the cut into beauty territory feels like an earned payoff rather than a jarring angle shift.

duration.reasoning: 1.7s is short-to-average for a technique beat, but this isn't really functioning as a technique beat anymore — the editor is using this slot as a bridge cut into the end card sequence, so the hold just needs to be long enough to register the transition (knife in motion, cut to plated result) without overstaying. The duration is determined less by the beat's nominal type and more by pacing into the end card: you want momentum, not a lingering pause before the beauty sequence opens up.

in_point.reasoning: Cutting in at 30.9s catches the knife mid-slice through the chicken — active motion at the in-point creates a dynamic cut that reads as a reveal (interior exposed, juicy cross-section appearing), which is exactly the emotional beat you want as the final "payoff" frame before the end card beauty shots. Starting a hair earlier would show setup; this frame is the knife breaking through, which is the money moment.
